abstract:
Foundation models, now powering most of the exciting applications in deep
learning, are almost universally based on the Transformer architecture and its
core attention module. Many subquadratic-time architectures such as linear
attention, gated convolution and recurrent models, and structured state space
models (SSMs) have been developed to address Transformers' computational
inefficiency on long sequences, but they have not performed as well as
attention on important modalities such as language. We identify that a key
weakness of such models is their inability to perform content-based reasoning,
and make several improvements. First, simply letting the SSM parameters be
functions of the input addresses their weakness with discrete modalities,
allowing the model to selectively propagate or forget information along the
sequence length dimension depending on the current token. Second, even though
this change prevents the use of efficient convolutions, we design a
hardware-aware parallel algorithm in recurrent mode. We integrate these
selective SSMs into a simplified end-to-end neural network architecture without
attention or even MLP blocks (Mamba). Mamba enjoys fast inference (5$\times$
higher throughput than Transformers) and linear scaling in sequence length, and
its performance improves on real data up to million-length sequences. As a
general sequence model backbone, Mamba achieves state-of-the-art performance
across several modalities such as language, audio, and genomics. On language
modeling, our Mamba-3B model outperforms Transformers of the same size and
matches Transformers twice its size, both in pretraining and downstream
evaluation.

keyInsights: Input-dependent selectivity in state space models is crucial for efficient long-context, content-aware reasoning in discrete modalities like language and DNA.
Hardware-aware kernel fusion and recomputation strategies are essential for making time-varying recurrent models computationally feasible and memory efficient on modern GPUs.

A simplified, homogenous architecture based solely on selective State Space Models can achieve Transformer-level quality across diverse modalities without requiring attention or traditional MLP blocks.
originalProblem: Transformer architecture's quadratic computational and memory scaling limits its application to very long sequences.
Existing sub-quadratic sequence models (including prior State Space Models) struggle with "content-based reasoning" and dynamic information selection.
Prior efficient models generally fail to match Transformer quality on dense modalities like language.
results: Matches Transformer-level performance in perplexity-vs-FLOPs scaling on language modeling and outperforms other attention-free models.
Solves synthetic tasks like selective copying and induction heads perfectly, demonstrating extrapolation capabilities up to 1 million tokens (4000x longer than training length).

Achieves 5x higher inference throughput than Transformers and is 20-40x faster than standard PyTorch scan implementations for its core operation, while maintaining memory efficiency comparable to FlashAttention-2.
solution: Introduces "Selective State Space Models" (S6) by making SSM parameters input-dependent to enable dynamic content-based reasoning.
Develops a hardware-aware "Selective Scan" algorithm that fuses operations into a single CUDA kernel and recomputes states for the backward pass, optimizing for speed and memory efficiency.
Proposes a simplified, homogenous "Mamba block" architecture that integrates the selective SSM, forming a unified block without separate attention or traditional MLP components.
paper_summary.summary: Mamba introduces Selective State Space Models (SSMs) to create a linear-time sequence model that matches Transformer performance while scaling linearly with sequence length. It demonstrates competitive quality on language, genomics, and audio tasks, achieving up to 5x higher inference throughput and significantly better long-context generalization than prior models.

title: Mamba: Linear-Time Sequence Modeling with Selective State Spaces

abstract: As inference-time scaling becomes critical for enhanced reasoning capabilities, it is increasingly becoming important to build models that are efficient to infer. We introduce Nemotron-H, a family of 8B and 56B/47B hybrid Mamba-Transformer models designed to reduce inference cost for a given accuracy level. To achieve this goal, we replace the majority of self-attention layers in the common Transformer model architecture with Mamba layers that perform constant computation and require constant memory per generated token. We show that Nemotron-H models offer either better or on-par accuracy compared to other similarly-sized state-of-the-art open-sourced Transformer models (e.g., Qwen-2.5-7B/72B and Llama-3.1-8B/70B), while being up to 3$\times$ faster at inference. To further increase inference speed and reduce the memory required at inference time, we created Nemotron-H-47B-Base from the 56B model using a new compression via pruning and distillation technique called MiniPuzzle. Nemotron-H-47B-Base achieves similar accuracy to the 56B model, but is 20% faster to infer. In addition, we introduce an FP8-based training recipe and show that it can achieve on par results with BF16-based training. This recipe is used to train the 56B model. We are releasing Nemotron-H base model checkpoints with support in Hugging Face and NeMo.
authors: et al. (100 additional authors not shown)

full_authors.full_name: et al. (100 additional authors not shown)

title: Nemotron-H: A Family of Accurate and Efficient Hybrid Mamba-Transformer Models

abstract: The attention mechanism in Transformers is an important primitive for accurate and scalable sequence modeling. Its quadratic-compute and linear-memory complexity however remain significant bottlenecks. Linear attention and state-space models enable linear-time, constant-memory sequence modeling and can moreover be trained efficiently through matmul-rich parallelization across sequence length. However, at their core these models are still RNNs, and thus their use of a fixed-size hidden state to model the context is a fundamental limitation. This paper develops log-linear attention, an attention mechanism that balances linear attention's efficiency and the expressiveness of softmax attention. Log-linear attention replaces the fixed-size hidden state with a logarithmically growing set of hidden states. We show that with a particular growth function, log-linear attention admits a similarly matmul-rich parallel form whose compute cost is log-linear in sequence length. Log-linear attention is a general framework and can be applied on top of existing linear attention variants. As case studies, we instantiate log-linear variants of two recent architectures -- Mamba-2 and Gated DeltaNet -- and find they perform well compared to their linear-time variants.

organization_info.name: Mohamed bin Zayed University of AI

keyInsights: Maintaining a logarithmically growing set of hidden states allows for a richer, multi-scale contextual representation, enabling better modeling of long-range dependencies without incurring quadratic computational costs.

The log-linear attention operator can be rigorously connected to structured matrix theory, specifically hierarchical matrices, providing a mathematical foundation for efficient parallel algorithms for both training and inference.

The log-linear attention framework is generalizable, allowing for the enhancement of existing efficient architectures like Mamba-2 and Gated DeltaNet by composing their inherent structured temporal masks with the proposed hierarchical mask.
originalProblem: Standard Transformer attention suffers from quadratic computational complexity (O(T^2)) and linear memory complexity (O(T)), limiting its application to very long sequences.

Existing efficient alternatives like linear attention and state-space models (SSMs) rely on a single, fixed-size hidden state, which can restrict their expressiveness and ability to model complex, multi-scale temporal dependencies or perform associative recall over extended contexts.
The need for an attention mechanism that avoids quadratic costs while overcoming the expressiveness limitations of purely linear models, to handle extensive context efficiently.
results: Achieved O(T log T) compute and O(log T) memory complexity for training and decoding, with custom Triton kernels demonstrating higher throughput than FlashAttention-2 (forward + backward pass) for sequence lengths beyond 8K on an H100 GPU.

Improved performance on synthetic tasks like Multi-Query Associative Recall (sometimes achieving >99% accuracy) and showed better long-range context utilization on language modeling tasks, including reduced per-position loss and improved Needle-In-A-Haystack scores.

Log-Linear Gated DeltaNet showed gains in WikiText perplexity and several zero-shot commonsense reasoning benchmarks compared to its linear counterpart, outperforming a layer-matched Transformer across all metrics.
solution: Replaces the single, fixed-size hidden state of linear attention with a set of hidden states whose size grows logarithmically with sequence length, using a Fenwick tree decomposition for multi-scale context representation.

Formulates the attention mechanism as a structured matrix multiplication involving a 'hierarchical matrix' ($M_H$), identified as a quasi-Hierarchically Off-Diagonal Low-Rank (HODLR) matrix, which enables efficient parallel computations.
Develops a chunkwise parallel scan algorithm, implemented with custom Triton kernels, for O(T log T) training and O(log T) decoding, suitable for modern hardware accelerators.
paper_summary.summary: Log-linear attention is presented as a novel mechanism combining O(T log T) computational complexity and O(log T) memory through a logarithmically growing set of hidden states. It improved performance on long-context tasks like Multi-Query Associative Recall and Needle-In-A-Haystack, and showed competitive or better throughput than FlashAttention-2 for sequences over 8K.

title: Log-Linear Attention

abstract: Effective reasoning is crucial to solving complex mathematical problems. Recent large language models (LLMs) have boosted performance by scaling test-time computation through long chain-of-thought reasoning. However, transformer-based models are inherently limited in extending context length due to their quadratic computational complexity and linear memory requirements. In this paper, we introduce a novel hybrid linear RNN reasoning model, M1, built on the Mamba architecture, which allows memory-efficient inference. Our approach leverages a distillation process from existing reasoning models and is further enhanced through RL training. Experimental results on the AIME and MATH benchmarks show that M1 not only outperforms previous linear RNN models but also matches the performance of state-of-the-art Deepseek R1 distilled reasoning models at a similar scale. We also compare our generation speed with a highly performant general purpose inference engine, vLLM, and observe more than a 3x speedup compared to a same size transformer. With throughput speedup, we are able to achieve higher accuracy compared to DeepSeek R1 distilled transformer reasoning models under a fixed generation time budget using self-consistency voting. Overall, we introduce a hybrid Mamba reasoning model and provide a more effective approach to scaling test-time generation using self-consistency or long chain of thought reasoning.

keyInsights: Hybrid Mamba architectures can achieve reasoning performance comparable to or slightly surpassing specialized distilled Transformer models on complex mathematical benchmarks.
Mamba's memory efficiency enables significant inference speedups (over 3x at large batch sizes and 2x for long sequence generation) compared to similarly-sized Transformer models.

The increased inference throughput of Mamba models can be directly leveraged to enhance reasoning accuracy by allowing more extensive test-time compute (e.g., self-consistency voting) within the same time budget.
originalProblem: Transformer-based Large Language Models (LLMs) suffer from quadratic computational complexity and linear memory requirements, hindering scalable reasoning tasks.
These limitations restrict the efficient handling of long Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning sequences and large batch sizes during inference.
There was a need to evaluate if alternative architectures, specifically Mamba, could maintain strong performance on specialized reasoning tasks compared to Transformers.
results: M1-3B achieved competitive reasoning scores, for instance, scoring 23.5% on AIME25 and 47.3% on OlympiadBench, often matching or slightly exceeding state-of-the-art distilled Transformer reasoning models.
The model demonstrated over a 3x speedup in inference throughput compared to a Llama-3.2-3B Transformer at large batch sizes and was at least 2x faster for long sequence generation.

Under a fixed time budget, M1 achieved comparable or higher reasoning accuracy than Transformer models by enabling a greater number of samples for self-consistency, effectively converting speed into enhanced performance.
solution: A hybrid linear RNN reasoning model, M1, was developed using the Mamba architecture with 6 interleaved attention layers among its 28 total layers.

A three-stage training process was implemented: distillation from a Transformer using reverse KL divergence, followed by multi-round supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on general math and reasoning-specific datasets.
The model underwent a final reinforcement learning (RL) phase using the GRPO loss function, with adaptations to remove the KL penalty and add an entropy bonus for policy refinement.
paper_summary.summary: M1 introduces a hybrid linear RNN reasoning model based on the Mamba architecture, addressing the scalability limitations of Transformers for complex reasoning tasks. It achieves competitive reasoning performance, demonstrates over 3x faster inference throughput than similarly sized Transformers, and effectively translates this speed into higher accuracy on mathematical benchmarks when leveraging test-time compute strategies like self-consistency.

title: M1: Towards Scalable Test-Time Compute with Mamba Reasoning Models

abstract: Transformers are the cornerstone of modern large language models, but their quadratic computational complexity limits efficiency in long-sequence processing. Recent advancements in Mamba, a state space model (SSM) with linear complexity, offer promising efficiency gains but suffer from unstable contextual learning and multitask generalization. Some works conduct layer-level hybrid structures that combine Transformer and Mamba layers, aiming to make full use of both advantages. This paper proposes TransMamba, a novel sequence-level hybrid framework that unifies Transformer and Mamba through shared parameter matrices (QKV and CBx), and thus could dynamically switch between attention and SSM mechanisms at different token lengths and layers. We design the Memory Converter to bridge Transformer and Mamba by converting attention outputs into SSM-compatible states, ensuring seamless information flow at TransPoints where the transformation happens. The TransPoint scheduling is also thoroughly explored for balancing effectiveness and efficiency. We conducted extensive experiments demonstrating that TransMamba achieves superior training efficiency and performance compared to single and hybrid baselines, and validated the deeper consistency between Transformer and Mamba paradigms at sequence level, offering a scalable solution for next-generation language modeling. Code and data are available at this https URL

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: A deeper consistency exists between Transformer's attention and Mamba's state space model, enabling them to share core parameter matrices within a unified architecture.
Dynamic, sequence-level adaptation between different mechanisms (Attention and SSM) using principled scheduling can effectively balance computational efficiency and robust contextual learning.
Lossless information transfer between distinct architectural components, facilitated by a Memory Converter, is critical for effective integration in hybrid models.
originalProblem: Transformers face quadratic computational complexity (O(T^2)) with sequence length, leading to high costs for very long sequences in both training and inference.
Initial Mamba models, while efficient with linear complexity, exhibit unstable contextual learning and suboptimal multitask generalization compared to Transformers.
Existing layer-level hybrid models suffer from rigid structural restrictions and often fail to dynamically adapt to varying sequence lengths and contextual demands.
results: TransMamba consistently outperforms pure Transformer, pure Mamba2, and naive Hybrid models across general English tasks and long-context benchmarks like LongBench-v2 (e.g., 38.76 for TransMamba-1.5B vs. 31.61 for Transformer).
Achieves up to a 25% reduction in average training time compared to Transformer models, demonstrating significant efficiency improvements consistent with theoretical FLOPs analysis.
Effective TransPoint scheduling, particularly layer-specific, broad-range, and fine-grained approaches, yields the best validation loss (2.898) and perplexity (1.813).
solution: Unifies Transformer and Mamba architectures through shared parameter matrices, allowing a single set of core parameters to serve as both QKV for Attention and CBx for SSM.
Introduces a sequence-level dynamic switching mechanism via a "TransPoint" within each layer, allowing the model to process different parts of the input sequence with either Attention or SSM.
Employs a lossless Memory Converter to seamlessly and without additional parameters transfer contextual information from the Attention mechanism to the initial hidden state of the Mamba SSM.
paper_summary.summary: A sequence-level hybrid Transformer-Mamba language model, TransMamba, unifies these architectures through shared parameters and dynamic switching, achieving improved performance on various NLP and long-text tasks while reducing training time by up to 25%.

title: TransMamba: A Sequence-Level Hybrid Transformer-Mamba Language Model

abstract:
Linear Transformers have gained attention as efficient alternatives to
standard Transformers, but their performance in retrieval and long-context
tasks has been limited. To address these limitations, recent work has explored
two distinct mechanisms: gating for adaptive memory control and the delta
update rule for precise memory modifications. We observe that these mechanisms
are complementary: gating enables rapid memory erasure while the delta rule
facilitates targeted updates. Building on this insight, we introduce the gated
delta rule and develop a parallel training algorithm optimized for modern
hardware. Our proposed architecture, Gated DeltaNet, consistently surpasses
existing models like Mamba2 and DeltaNet across multiple benchmarks, including
language modeling, common-sense reasoning, in-context retrieval, length
extrapolation, and long-context understanding. We further enhance performance
by developing hybrid architectures that combine Gated DeltaNet layers with
sliding window attention or Mamba2 layers, achieving both improved training
efficiency and superior task performance.

keyInsights: The scalar gating term from Mamba2, enabling rapid memory clearance, and the delta rule from DeltaNet, facilitating precise, selective updates, are complementary for effective memory management in sequence models.
The proposed gated delta rule can be interpreted within an online learning framework, where the gating term acts as an adaptive weight decay in the SGD update for the fast weight matrix.

Hybrid architectures that combine the gated delta rule with other token mixing mechanisms, such as sliding window attention (SWA) or Mamba2 layers, can leverage distinct strengths for enhanced performance and efficiency.
originalProblem: Mamba2's uniform decay mechanism (alpha_t) leads to inefficient information retention and "memory collisions" for tasks requiring long-term memory or precise retrieval of older, specific information.

DeltaNet, while strong at selective updates, lacks a mechanism for rapidly clearing large chunks of irrelevant or outdated information, hindering performance in tasks with context switches or noise filtering.
Existing efficient linear Transformers struggle to effectively manage complex memory for long-context understanding and precise retrieval while maintaining hardware efficiency.
results: Gated DeltaNet consistently outperforms Mamba2 and DeltaNet across language modeling, common-sense reasoning, and both synthetic and real-world in-context retrieval benchmarks.

On S-NIAH benchmarks, Gated DeltaNet shows less degradation in precise information retention than Mamba2 and demonstrates superior complex pattern memorization, while its gating facilitates filtering where DeltaNet struggles.

The model achieves the lowest perplexity among recurrent models in length extrapolation up to 20K tokens and demonstrates advantages in long-context understanding on the LongBench suite, particularly in retrieval and few-shot in-context learning.

Gated DeltaNet maintains hardware efficiency, with training throughput comparable to DeltaNet, and its hybrid variants (GatedDeltaNet-H1/-H2) demonstrate strong throughput across various sequence lengths.
solution: A novel "gated delta rule" is introduced, unifying Mamba2's adaptive decay (alpha_t) with DeltaNet's targeted update (beta_t) to enable flexible control over memory: rapid clearance or precise, selective updates.
A hardware-efficient chunkwise parallel training algorithm is developed, extending previous work on DeltaNet to efficiently implement the new gated delta rule, preserving linear-time complexity.

Two hybrid architectures are proposed: GatedDeltaNet-H1 combines Gated DeltaNet layers with Sliding Window Attention, and GatedDeltaNet-H2 stacks Mamba2, Gated DeltaNet, and SWA layers for multi-layered sequence modeling.
paper_summary.summary: Gated Delta Networks introduce a "gated delta rule" that unifies Mamba2's adaptive decay with DeltaNet's targeted memory updates, leading to a more robust and flexible memory management mechanism for efficient sequence models. This approach consistently outperforms prior linear recurrent models across language modeling, retrieval, and long-context understanding benchmarks while maintaining hardware efficiency.

title: Gated Delta Networks: Improving Mamba2 with Delta Rule

abstract: Linear sequence modeling methods, such as linear attention, state space modeling, and linear RNNs, offer significant efficiency improvements by reducing the complexity of training and inference. However, these methods typically compress the entire input sequence into a single fixed-size memory state, which leads to suboptimal performance on recall-intensive tasks. To address this limitation, we introduce a novel architecture called Mixture-of-Memories (MoM). MoM utilizes multiple independent memory states, with a router network directing input tokens to specific memory states. This approach greatly enhances the overall memory capacity while minimizing memory interference. MoM serves as a general framework that can be seamlessly combined with diverse memory update mechanisms across linear models. As a result, MoM performs exceptionally well on recall-intensive tasks, surpassing existing linear sequence modeling techniques. Despite incorporating multiple memory states, the computation of each memory state remains linear in complexity, allowing MoM to retain the linear-complexity advantage during training, while constant-complexity during inference. Our experimental results show that MoM outperforms current linear sequence models on downstream language tasks, particularly recall-intensive tasks, and even achieves performance comparable to Transformer models. The code is released at this https URL and is also released as a part of this https URL.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou)

keyInsights: Separating memory into distinct, independently updated units is more effective at mitigating memory interference and expanding capacity than simply increasing the size of a single memory state.
A dynamic router allows for specialization, where different memory states can learn to handle distinct types of information, contributing to better information retention and recall.

The mixture-of-memories approach successfully bridges the performance gap with Transformers on challenging recall-intensive and long-context tasks, demonstrating that high performance and efficiency can coexist in sequence models.
originalProblem: Existing linear sequence models suffer from limited memory capacity due to compressing entire input sequences into a single fixed-size memory state.
This single memory state is prone to memory interference, where new information overwrites or degrades previously stored representations, hindering performance on tasks requiring precise recall.

These limitations prevent linear models from matching the performance of Transformer architectures, particularly for complex tasks requiring extensive contextual understanding, despite their efficiency benefits.
results: MoM significantly outperforms other linear models on recall-intensive tasks (e.g., SQuAD, NQ), achieving an average score of 36.04 for the 1.3B model, comparable to Transformer++'s 37.31, and shows the highest average performance on LongBench (15.64) for long context comprehension.

It maintains linear time complexity for inference and constant memory usage with respect to sequence length, providing substantial efficiency advantages over Transformer++ and preventing out-of-memory errors on long sequences.

Ablation studies confirm that using multiple *separated* memory segments yields better performance than increasing the capacity of a *single* memory state, and MoM exhibits superior length extrapolation capabilities up to 32K context lengths compared to baselines.
solution: MoM introduces multiple independent memory states, each capable of storing different aspects of the input sequence, managed by a router network.

A router selects a subset of memories for activation, updating only these chosen memories with specialized key-value projections of the input, while inactive memories remain unchanged to prevent interference.
A 'shared memory' component is included to capture global context, complementing the specialized memories, and the activated memories are mixed into a single representation for output generation.
paper_summary.summary: The Mixture-of-Memories (MoM) architecture replaces a single recurrent state with multiple, independent memory states and a routing mechanism, enhancing linear sequence models' ability to retain information over long sequences. This design enables performance on recall-intensive tasks comparable to Transformer models while maintaining linear time complexity during training and constant time inference.

title: MoM: Linear Sequence Modeling with Mixture-of-Memories

abstract:
Recent advancements have demonstrated that the performance of large language
models (LLMs) can be significantly enhanced by scaling computational resources
at test time. A common strategy involves generating multiple Chain-of-Thought
(CoT) trajectories and aggregating their outputs through various selection
mechanisms. This raises a fundamental question: can models with lower
complexity leverage their superior generation throughput to outperform
similarly sized Transformers for a fixed computational budget? To address this
question and overcome the lack of strong subquadratic reasoners, we distill
pure and hybrid Mamba models from pretrained Transformers. Trained on only 8
billion tokens, our distilled models show strong performance and scaling on
mathematical reasoning datasets while being much faster at inference for large
batches and long sequences. Despite the zero-shot performance hit due to
distillation, both pure and hybrid Mamba models can scale their coverage and
accuracy performance past their Transformer teacher models under fixed time
budgets, opening a new direction for scaling inference compute.

keyInsights: Under fixed compute budgets, faster inference enables more sample generations, potentially outperforming slower but more accurate models
Cross-architecture distillation can successfully transfer reasoning capabilities while maintaining efficiency advantages
Post-distillation supervised fine-tuning further enhances model performance
originalProblem: Traditional Transformer architectures face significant computational scaling challenges during inference
Knowledge distillation across different architectural paradigms remains challenging, especially for complex reasoning tasks
results: Achieved 2.5-4x faster inference compared to baseline Transformers
Demonstrated superior performance under fixed time budgets through increased sample generation
Successfully maintained reasoning capabilities while reducing computational complexity
Established viability of efficient alternatives to Transformers for complex reasoning tasks
solution: Developed two novel distillation approaches: pure Mamba ("Llamba") and hybrid Transformer-Mamba ("MambaInLlama")
Implemented modified MOHAWK distillation procedure with three-stage process for efficient knowledge transfer
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from multiple leading institutions demonstrate how distilled Mamba-based models can achieve 2.5-4x faster inference than Transformers while maintaining or improving performance on mathematical reasoning tasks, establishing new approaches for scaling inference compute through efficient architecture alternatives.

title: Thinking Slow, Fast: Scaling Inference Compute with Distilled Reasoners

abstract:
State space models (SSMs) have emerged as an efficient alternative to
Transformer models for language modeling, offering linear computational
complexity and constant memory usage as context length increases. However,
despite their efficiency in handling long contexts, recent studies have shown
that SSMs, such as Mamba models, generally underperform compared to
Transformers in long-context understanding tasks. To address this significant
shortfall and achieve both efficient and accurate long-context understanding,
we propose LongMamba, a training-free technique that significantly enhances the
long-context capabilities of Mamba models. LongMamba builds on our discovery
that the hidden channels in Mamba can be categorized into local and global
channels based on their receptive field lengths, with global channels primarily
responsible for long-context capability. These global channels can become the
key bottleneck as the input context lengthens. Specifically, when input lengths
largely exceed the training sequence length, global channels exhibit
limitations in adaptively extend their receptive fields, leading to Mamba's
poor long-context performance. The key idea of LongMamba is to mitigate the
hidden state memory decay in these global channels by preventing the
accumulation of unimportant tokens in their memory. This is achieved by first
identifying critical tokens in the global channels and then applying token
filtering to accumulate only those critical tokens. Through extensive
benchmarking across synthetic and real-world long-context scenarios, LongMamba
sets a new standard for Mamba's long-context performance, significantly
extending its operational range without requiring additional training. Our code
is available at this https URL

keyInsights: Mamba's architecture contains distinct local and global attention channels
Global channels are primarily responsible for long-range dependencies
Selective filtering of less important tokens can mitigate hidden state decay
Training-free approaches can effectively extend context length
originalProblem: Mamba models underperform Transformers in long-context understanding tasks
Global channels in Mamba fail to extend their receptive fields beyond training sequence length
Hidden state memory decay limits the model's ability to maintain long-range dependencies
results: Significant improvements over vanilla Mamba and DeciMamba across multiple benchmarks
Successfully applied to pure SSMs and hybrid Transformer-SSM architectures
Minimal latency overhead while maintaining computational efficiency
Demonstrated scalability to larger models like Falcon-Mamba 7B
solution: Classification system to identify local and global channels based on cumulative decay
Token filtering mechanism for global channels to enlarge their receptive fields
Adaptive filtering threshold that scales with target context length
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Georgia Tech and NVIDIA develop LongMamba, a training-free method that enhances Mamba models' ability to process long sequences by identifying and optimizing global attention channels, achieving consistent performance improvements across multiple benchmarks while maintaining the model's computational efficiency.

title: LongMamba: Enhancing Mamba's Long Context Capabilities via Training-Free  Receptive Field Enlargement

abstract: Linear Recurrent Neural Networks (linear RNNs) have emerged as competitive alternatives to Transformers for sequence modeling, offering efficient training and linear-time inference. However, existing architectures face a fundamental trade-off between expressivity and efficiency, dictated by the structure of their state-transition matrices. Diagonal matrices, used in models such as Mamba, GLA, or mLSTM, yield fast runtime but have limited expressivity. To address this, recent architectures such as DeltaNet and RWKV-7 adopted a diagonal plus rank--1 structure, which allows simultaneous token and channel mixing, improving associative recall and, as recently shown, state-tracking when allowing state-transition matrices to have negative eigenvalues. Building on the interpretation of DeltaNet's recurrence as performing one step of online gradient descent per token on an associative recall loss, we introduce DeltaProduct, which instead takes multiple ($n_h$) steps per token. This naturally leads to diagonal plus rank--$n_h$ state-transition matrices, formed as products of $n_h$ generalized Householder transformations, providing a tunable mechanism to balance expressivity and efficiency. We provide a detailed theoretical characterization of the state-tracking capability of DeltaProduct in finite precision, showing how it improves by increasing $n_h$. Our extensive experiments demonstrate that DeltaProduct outperforms DeltaNet in both state-tracking and language modeling, while also showing significantly improved length extrapolation capabilities.

keyInsights: Increasing the number of Householder products ('n_h') in a single layer is more effective for enhancing state-tracking capabilities and length extrapolation than increasing the number of layers with fixed 'n_h=1'.
The ability to form reflections and rotations through Householder products, specifically by utilizing the extended eigenvalue range [-1, 1], is critical for solving complex group word problems.
DeltaProduct can exploit mathematical isomorphisms, such as S4 and A5 mapping to 3D rotations, to solve these group problems with fewer Householder products than theoretically derived constructions.
originalProblem: Existing efficient linear RNNs with diagonal state-transition matrices exhibit significant expressivity limitations for complex state-tracking and formal language recognition.
The quadratic computational complexity of Transformers with respect to sequence length restricts their scalability for processing very long sequences.
Developing linear RNNs with both high expressivity and guaranteed recurrence stability has been a persistent challenge.
results: DeltaProduct achieved robust extrapolation and high accuracy on challenging group word problems (e.g., S3, S4, A5, S5) with 'n_h' as low as 2, significantly outperforming DeltaNet with 'n_h=1' across various layer configurations.

The model demonstrated a sharp improvement in length extrapolation performance on language modeling datasets (CodeParrot, OpenThoughts, TriviaQA) when increasing 'n_h' from 1 to 2, indicating enhanced generalization to sequences beyond training context.

Scaling analysis showed that DeltaProduct models achieved lower training perplexity and better average accuracy on language benchmarks compared to parameter-matched DeltaNet models, affirming the efficiency of increasing expressivity via 'n_h'.
solution: DeltaProduct proposes a novel recurrence mechanism that updates the hidden state using a product of 'n_h' generalized Householder transformations per token, which is conceptualized as multiple online gradient descent steps.
The architecture introduces 'n_h' as a tunable parameter, allowing for a smooth interpolation between the limited expressivity of diagonal matrices and the higher expressivity of dense matrices.
The design inherently maintains a spectral norm of at most 1 for the state-transition matrix, guaranteeing recurrence stability even on very long sequences.
paper_summary.summary: DeltaProduct introduces a linear RNN architecture that improves state-tracking and length extrapolation by employing products of generalized Householder transformations, which allows for tunable expressivity and guarantees recurrence stability. The model exhibits enhanced performance on complex group word problems and significantly better generalization to longer sequences in language modeling tasks.

title: DeltaProduct: Improving State-Tracking in Linear RNNs via Householder Products

abstract:
Selective state-space models (SSMs) like Mamba overcome some of the
shortcomings of Transformers, such as quadratic computational complexity with
sequence length and large inference-time memory requirements from the key-value
cache. Moreover, recent studies have shown that SSMs can match or exceed the
language modeling capabilities of Transformers, making them an attractive
alternative. In a controlled setting (e.g., same data), however, studies so far
have only presented small scale experiments comparing SSMs to Transformers. To
understand the strengths and weaknesses of these architectures at larger
scales, we present a direct comparison between 8B-parameter Mamba, Mamba-2, and
Transformer models trained on the same datasets of up to 3.5T tokens. We also
compare these models to a hybrid architecture consisting of 43% Mamba-2, 7%
attention, and 50% MLP layers (Mamba-2-Hybrid). Using a diverse set of tasks,
we answer the question of whether Mamba models can match Transformers at larger
training budgets. Our results show that while pure SSMs match or exceed
Transformers on many tasks, they lag behind Transformers on tasks which require
strong copying or in-context learning abilities (e.g., 5-shot MMLU, Phonebook)
or long-context reasoning. In contrast, we find that the 8B Mamba-2-Hybrid
exceeds the 8B Transformer on all 12 standard tasks we evaluated (+2.65 points
on average) and is predicted to be up to 8x faster when generating tokens at
inference time. To validate long-context capabilities, we provide additional
experiments evaluating variants of the Mamba-2-Hybrid and Transformer extended
to support 16K, 32K, and 128K sequences. On an additional 23 long-context
tasks, the hybrid model continues to closely match or exceed the Transformer on
average. To enable further study, we release the checkpoints as well as the
code used to train our models as part of NVIDIA's Megatron-LM project.

keyInsights: Pure Structured State Space Models (SSMs) like Mamba and Mamba-2 excel in computational efficiency but struggle with tasks requiring precise long-range information copying.
A hybrid architecture, combining 90% Mamba-2 layers with 10% self-attention layers, effectively mitigates the copying deficit while retaining much of the efficiency.
Hybrid Mamba-2 models exhibit strong generalization capabilities for context lengths far beyond their training data, maintaining performance up to 180K tokens after training on 32K tokens.
originalProblem: Transformer models, while effective, face quadratic scaling of self-attention with sequence length, leading to high computational costs and memory requirements for long contexts during training and inference.
The substantial memory footprint of key-value caches in Transformers presents a bottleneck for deploying large models, particularly in applications with long input sequences.
There is a need for language model architectures that offer improved efficiency without sacrificing performance on complex language understanding tasks.
results: The Mamba-2-Hybrid model achieved a 2.65 percentage point average improvement over pure Transformer models on standard language tasks, with notable gains on reasoning tasks like MATH (+4.7 points) and GSM8K (+3.5 points).

Pure Mamba and Mamba-2 models showed significantly degraded performance on verbatim copying tasks (e.g., Phonebook Lookup) beyond a few thousand tokens, while the Mamba-2-Hybrid maintained accuracy up to approximately 5K tokens.
Mamba-2-Hybrid models demonstrated up to 8x faster token generation during inference compared to Transformers, with the speedup increasing with longer input contexts (e.g., 7x at 128K tokens).
solution: Researchers conducted a comprehensive empirical study comparing Mamba-based language models (Mamba, Mamba-2) with Transformer models at 8 billion parameters, trained on up to 3.5 trillion tokens.
They investigated a novel Mamba-2-Hybrid architecture that combines Mamba-2 layers with a small percentage (optimized to 10%) of traditional self-attention layers.

All models were trained and evaluated under controlled conditions, using identical datasets, vocabulary sizes, training hyperparameters, and a comprehensive suite of benchmarks including standard language understanding and specialized long-context tasks.
paper_summary.summary: This empirical study evaluates Mamba-based language models, including a novel Mamba-2-Hybrid architecture, at 8 billion parameters against Transformer models. The Mamba-2-Hybrid model combines efficient state-space layers with a small fraction of self-attention layers, outperforming pure Transformers on standard language tasks and demonstrating superior long-context generalization and significantly faster inference speeds, particularly for very long sequences.

title: An Empirical Study of Mamba-based Language Models

abstract: State Space Models (SSMs) are emerging as a compelling alternative to Transformers because of their consistent memory usage and high performance. Despite this, scaling up SSMs on cloud services or limited-resource devices is challenging due to their storage requirements and computational power. To overcome this, quantizing SSMs with low bit-width data formats can reduce model size and benefit from hardware acceleration. As SSMs are prone to quantization-induced errors, recent efforts have focused on optimizing a particular model or bit-width for efficiency without sacrificing performance. However, distinct bit-width configurations are essential for different scenarios, like W4A8 for boosting large-batch decoding speed, and W4A16 for enhancing generation speed in short prompt applications for a single user. To this end, we present Quamba2, compatible with W8A8, W4A8, and W4A16 for both Mamba1 and Mamba2 backbones, addressing the growing demand for SSM deployment on various platforms. Based on the channel order preserving and activation persistence of SSMs, we propose an offline approach to quantize inputs of a linear recurrence in 8-bit by sorting and clustering for input $x$, combined with a per-state-group quantization for input-dependent parameters $B$ and $C$. To ensure compute-invariance in the SSM output, we rearrange weights offline according to the clustering sequence. The experiments show that Quamba2-8B outperforms two state-of-the-art SSM quantization methods and delivers 1.3$\times$ and 3$\times$ speed-ups in the pre-filling and generation stages, respectively, while offering 4$\times$ memory reduction with only a $1.6\%$ average accuracy drop. The evaluation on MMLU shows the generalizability and robustness of our framework. The code and quantized models will be released at: this https URL.

organization_info.name: The University of Texas at Austin

keyInsights: SSM activation properties, such as channel order preservation and activation persistence, provide crucial structural information that can be exploited for developing effective SSM-specific quantization strategies.

Fine-grained quantization using specialized scaling factors for clustered input activations and specific state groups is critical for mitigating error sensitivity in SSMs and achieving high accuracy at low bit-widths.

Balancing aggressive efficiency with robust generalizability, especially for multi-step reasoning tasks, can be achieved through mixed-precision approaches that selectively apply higher precision to sensitive model blocks.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs), including newer State Space Models (SSMs) like Mamba, require substantial computational power and memory, hindering widespread deployment, especially on resource-constrained edge devices.

Existing post-training quantization (PTQ) techniques developed for Transformers are often ineffective for SSMs due to the extreme sensitivity of their linear recurrence mechanism to quantization errors.
Aggressive quantization can impair the generalization capabilities of models, particularly on complex multi-step reasoning tasks, a challenge not fully explored or addressed for SSMs.
results:
Quamba2-W4A8 achieves 3.89
× memory reduction, 1.39
× faster prefilling, and 3.05
× faster generation for Mamba2-8B on an A5000, and uniquely enables deployment of Mamba2-8B on an Nvidia Orin Nano 8G (edge device) at 13 tokens/second where FP16 models fail due to out-of-memory.

On five zero-shot tasks, W8A8 Quamba2-8B achieves 69.9% accuracy (only 1.6% drop from FP16), outperforming previous SOTA SSM quantization methods, while W4A{8/16}-mixed precision improves MMLU accuracy by 2.9% over pure W4A8.

Quamba2 models consistently demonstrate higher throughput-per-energy (tokens per Gigawatt) on A5000 and enable significantly lower energy consumption for Mamba2-8B on Orin Nano, confirming energy efficiency for both cloud and edge deployments.
solution: Quamba2 employs novel quantization techniques tailored for SSMs, including "sort-and-cluster" for input activations ($x$) and "per-state-group quantization" for parameters $B$ and $C$, enhancing precision at low bit-widths.

The framework features a hardware-aware system design with cluster-aware weight reordering, offline Hadamard matrix fusion, and optimized CUDA kernels for Mamba blocks, supporting W8A8, W4A8, and W4A16 configurations.

It implements head-to-toe quantization for all model layers and introduces W4A*X*-mixed precision, using an evolutionary search to assign higher precision to performance-sensitive blocks to maintain generalizability.
paper_summary.summary: Quamba2, a post-training quantization framework, enables robust and scalable deployment of selective State Space Models (SSMs) like Mamba1 and Mamba2 by supporting various bit-width configurations (W8A8, W4A8, W4A16). This framework achieves substantial speed-ups and memory reductions, maintains accuracy, and allows for the deployment of 8B models on edge devices where full-precision models fail.

title: Quamba2: A Robust and Scalable Post-training Quantization Framework for Selective State Space Models

abstract:
Recent Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable
performance but face deployment challenges due to their quadratic computational
complexity, growing Key-Value cache requirements, and reliance on separate
vision encoders. We propose mmMamba, a framework for developing
linear-complexity native multimodal state space models through progressive
distillation from existing MLLMs using moderate academic computational
resources. Our approach enables the direct conversion of trained decoder-only
MLLMs to linear-complexity architectures without requiring pre-trained
RNN-based LLM or vision encoders. We propose an seeding strategy to carve Mamba
from trained Transformer and a three-stage distillation recipe, which can
effectively transfer the knowledge from Transformer to Mamba while preserving
multimodal capabilities. Our method also supports flexible hybrid architectures
that combine Transformer and Mamba layers for customizable
efficiency-performance trade-offs. Distilled from the Transformer-based
decoder-only HoVLE, mmMamba-linear achieves competitive performance against
existing linear and quadratic-complexity VLMs, while mmMamba-hybrid further
improves performance significantly, approaching HoVLE's capabilities. At 103K
tokens, mmMamba-linear demonstrates 20.6$\times$ speedup and 75.8% GPU memory
reduction compared to HoVLE, while mmMamba-hybrid achieves 13.5$\times$ speedup
and 60.2% memory savings. Code and models are released at
this https URL
title: Multimodal Mamba: Decoder-only Multimodal State Space Model via  Quadratic to Linear Distillation

abstract: State-space models (SSMs) have recently demonstrated competitive performance to transformers at large-scale language modeling benchmarks while achieving linear time and memory complexity as a function of sequence length. Mamba, a recently released SSM model, shows impressive performance in both language modeling and long sequence processing tasks. Simultaneously, mixture-of-expert (MoE) models have shown remarkable performance while significantly reducing the compute and latency costs of inference at the expense of a larger memory footprint. In this paper, we present BlackMamba, a novel architecture that combines the Mamba SSM with MoE to obtain the benefits of both. We demonstrate that BlackMamba performs competitively against both Mamba and transformer baselines, and outperforms in inference and training FLOPs. We fully train and open-source 340M/1.5B and 630M/2.8B BlackMamba models on 300B tokens of a custom dataset. We show that BlackMamba inherits and combines both of the benefits of SSM and MoE architectures, combining linear-complexity generation from SSM with cheap and fast inference from MoE. We release all weights, checkpoints, and inference code open-source. Inference code at: this https URL

keyInsights: The synergistic integration of Mamba's linear complexity and constant-time generation with MoE's FLOP-efficient inference creates a superior trade-off in performance and resource usage for LLMs.
Effective expert load balancing through a refined Sinkhorn initialization is crucial for realizing the full efficiency benefits of MoE at scale.
The combined SSM-MoE architecture empirically demonstrates strong scaling potential, offering a viable and more efficient alternative to dense Transformer models for large-scale language tasks.
originalProblem: Transformer architectures suffer from quadratic computational and memory complexity with respect to sequence length, limiting context windows and increasing autoregressive generation costs.
While State-Space Models (SSMs) and Mixture-of-Experts (MoEs) offer distinct efficiency advantages, their large-scale combination had not been thoroughly explored to create more scalable LLMs.
results: BlackMamba 631M/2.8B achieved competitive performance on downstream language tasks (average score 0.466), outperforming Mamba 343M (0.417) and closely competing with Pythia 1.4B (0.474).
The architecture required significantly fewer training FLOPs; BlackMamba 631M/2.8B used 1.2e21 FLOPs, compared to Pythia 1.4B (3.2e21 FLOPs) and Pythia 2.8B (6.1e21 FLOPs) for comparable quality.

BlackMamba demonstrated significantly faster autoregressive generation latency and maintained a constant memory footprint for arbitrary sequence lengths, a key advantage over dense Transformers and Mamba models.
solution: BlackMamba combines a Mamba block to handle sequence processing with linear complexity and an MoE layer for sparse, efficient capacity scaling within each architectural block.
A novel initialization for the Sinkhorn routing algorithm was developed to ensure efficient expert load balancing, reducing convergence iterations during training.
Multi-billion parameter BlackMamba models (e.g., 2.8 billion total parameters) were trained on a custom 300 billion token dataset, and all artifacts were open-sourced.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Zyphra introduced BlackMamba, a novel large language model architecture that integrates State-Space Models (Mamba) with Mixture-of-Experts (MoE), demonstrating competitive performance at multi-billion parameter scales with significantly reduced training FLOPs and constant memory autoregressive generation.

title: BlackMamba: Mixture of Experts for State-Space Models

abstract: Large pre-trained models have achieved outstanding results in sequence modeling. The Transformer block and its attention mechanism have been the main drivers of the success of these models. Recently, alternative architectures, such as Selective Structured State Space Models (SSMs), have been proposed to address the inefficiencies of Transformers. This paper explores the compression of SSM-based models, particularly Mamba and its hybrids. We study the sensitivity of these models to the removal of selected components at different granularities to reduce the model size and computational overhead, thus improving their efficiency while maintaining accuracy. The proposed solutions, collectively referred to as Mamba-Shedder, achieve a speedup of up to 1.4x during inference, demonstrating that model efficiency can be improved by eliminating several redundancies with minimal impact on the overall model performance. The code is available at this https URL.
title: Mamba-Shedder: Post-Transformer Compression for Efficient Selective Structured State Space Models

abstract:
We introduce Llamba, a family of efficient recurrent language models
distilled from Llama-3.x into the Mamba architecture. The series includes
Llamba-1B, Llamba-3B, and Llamba-8B, which achieve higher inference throughput
and handle significantly larger batch sizes than Transformer-based models while
maintaining comparable benchmark performance. Furthermore, Llamba demonstrates
the effectiveness of cross-architecture distillation using MOHAWK (Bick et al.,
2024), achieving these results with less than 0.1% of the training data
typically used for models of similar size. To take full advantage of their
efficiency, we provide an optimized implementation of Llamba for
resource-constrained devices such as smartphones and edge platforms, offering a
practical and memory-efficient alternative to Transformers. Overall, Llamba
improves the tradeoff between speed, memory efficiency, and performance, making
high-quality language models more accessible.
title: Llamba: Scaling Distilled Recurrent Models for Efficient Language  Processing

abstract: The typical Selective State-Space Model (SSM) used in Mamba addresses several limitations of Transformers, such as the quadratic computational complexity with respect to sequence length and the significant memory requirements during inference due to the key-value (KV) cache. However, the increasing size of Mamba models continues to pose challenges for training and deployment, particularly due to their substantial computational demands during both training and inference. In this work, we introduce $\texttt{Bi-Mamba}$, a scalable and powerful 1-bit Mamba architecture designed to enable more efficient large language models (LLMs), with model sizes of 780M, 1.3B, and 2.7B parameters. $\texttt{Bi-Mamba}$ models are trained from scratch on a standard LLM-scale dataset using an autoregressive distillation loss. Extensive experiments on language modeling benchmarks demonstrate that $\texttt{Bi-Mamba}$ achieves performance comparable to its full-precision (FP16 or BF16) counterparts, while outperforming post-training binarization (PTB) Mamba and binarization-aware training (BAT) Transformer baselines. Moreover, $\texttt{Bi-Mamba}$ drastically reduces memory usage and computational cost compared to the original Mamba. Our work pioneers a new line of linear-complexity LLMs under low-bit representation and provides the way for the design of specialized hardware optimized for efficient 1-bit Mamba-based models. Code and the pre-trained weights are available at this https URL.

organization_info.name: Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence

title: Bi-Mamba: Towards Accurate 1-Bit State Space Models

abstract:
Recent work has shown that attention-based language models excel at recall,
the ability to ground generations in tokens previously seen in context.
However, the efficiency of attention-based models is bottle-necked during
inference by the KV-cache's aggressive memory consumption. In this work, we
explore whether we can improve language model efficiency (e.g. by reducing
memory consumption) without compromising on recall. By applying experiments and
theory to a broad set of architectures, we identify a key tradeoff between a
model's state size and recall ability. We show that efficient alternatives to
attention (e.g. H3, Mamba, RWKV) maintain a fixed-size recurrent state, but
struggle at recall. We propose BASED a simple architecture combining linear and
sliding window attention. By varying BASED window size and linear attention
feature dimension, we can dial the state size and traverse the pareto frontier
of the recall-memory tradeoff curve, recovering the full quality of attention
on one end and the small state size of attention-alternatives on the other. We
train language models up to 1.3b parameters and show that BASED matches the
strongest sub-quadratic models (e.g. Mamba) in perplexity and outperforms them
on real-world recall-intensive tasks by 6.22 accuracy points. Implementations
of linear attention are often less efficient than optimized standard attention
implementations. To make BASED competitive, we develop IO-aware algorithms that
enable 24x higher throughput on language generation than FlashAttention-2, when
generating 1024 tokens using 1.3b parameter models. Code for this work is
provided at: this https URL

keyInsights: A model's recurrent state size during inference is fundamentally linked to its capacity for associative recall, establishing a core recall-memory tradeoff.

Hybrid architectures that judiciously combine global linear attention with local exact attention can significantly expand the Pareto frontier, offering a better balance of recall quality and efficiency.

The 2nd-order Taylor approximation of softmax provides an effective feature map for linear attention, enabling substantial recall capacity without increasing parameter count, and hardware-aware algorithm design is critical for realizing theoretical efficiency gains.
originalProblem: Transformer-based LLMs face severe efficiency bottlenecks, exhibiting quadratic computational complexity and high memory consumption (KV-cache) during inference, which limits long-context applications.
Existing efficient alternatives (e.g., Mamba, H3) often struggle with recall-intensive tasks despite their throughput gains, failing to effectively capture long-range dependencies.
A fundamental tradeoff exists between a model's recurrent state size (memory) and its capacity for associative recall, with current architectures not optimally balancing these aspects.
results: BASED achieves up to 24 times higher throughput for next token generation compared to FlashAttention-2 and is 56% faster for prompt prefill than FlashAttention-2, and 44% faster than Mamba.
On recall-intensive tasks like information extraction and question answering, BASED consistently outperforms Mamba by an average of 10.36 accuracy points at the 1.3 billion parameter scale.

The architecture maintains competitive overall perplexity on the Pile dataset and performs comparably to Transformer++ and Mamba on general natural language understanding benchmarks, while also demonstrating robustness in DNA modeling tasks.
solution: Introduces BASED (Block-wise and Segmented Recurrent Attention), a hybrid architecture combining a 2nd-order Taylor series approximation for linear attention and local exact attention with small sliding windows.
Employs Taylor Linear Attention to enable global context interaction with a fixed-size recurrent state, where the state size can be tuned by projecting queries and keys to a smaller dimension.
Integrates small, hardware-optimized sliding windows (TCWINDOW) to perform local exact attention, crucial for precise local token shifts and comparisons while maximizing GPU tensor core utilization.
Develops IO-aware CUDA algorithms for both prefill and decoding stages of BASED components to significantly reduce memory transfer costs and maximize real-world throughput on GPUs.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Stanford and the University of Buffalo developed BASED, a hybrid language model architecture combining Taylor linear attention and hardware-optimized sliding window attention. This approach efficiently improves recall in long contexts while achieving up to 24 times higher inference throughput compared to FlashAttention-2 and significantly outperforming Mamba on recall-intensive tasks.

title: Simple linear attention language models balance the recall-throughput  tradeoff

abstract: While the Mamba architecture demonstrates superior inference efficiency and competitive performance on short-context natural language processing (NLP) tasks, empirical evidence suggests its capacity to comprehend long contexts is limited compared to transformer-based models. In this study, we investigate the long-context efficiency issues of the Mamba models and propose ReMamba, which enhances Mamba's ability to comprehend long contexts. ReMamba incorporates selective compression and adaptation techniques within a two-stage re-forward process, incurring minimal additional inference costs overhead. Experimental results on the LongBench and L-Eval benchmarks demonstrate ReMamba's efficacy, improving over the baselines by 3.2 and 1.6 points, respectively, and attaining performance almost on par with same-size transformer models.

keyInsights: Mamba's fixed-size state space is insufficient for retaining critical information from very long sequences, leading to significant performance degradation that even crude compression methods can sometimes mitigate.
Intelligent identification and selective retention of important information from long contexts can effectively combat the 'forgetting' problem without compromising Mamba's inherent efficiency.

Dynamically scaling the state space update parameter (∆) based on information importance provides a differentiable and efficient mechanism for Mamba to adapt to and prioritize compressed, crucial long-context features.
originalProblem: Transformer models, dominant in LLMs, face quadratic computational complexity and linear memory consumption with increasing sequence length, limiting their application in very long contexts.

While Mamba offers linear time complexity and constant memory, it suffers from empirical performance degradation on long-context tasks due to its fixed-size state space 'forgetting' distant information.
Existing Mamba-specific context extension methods or hybrid architectures either reintroduce computational inefficiencies or prove insufficiently effective on challenging long-context benchmarks.
results: ReMamba achieved an average score of 27.86 on LongBench-E (6k tokens), representing a 3.23-point improvement over the Mamba baseline (24.63) and approaching Llama-3b's performance.
The model consistently outperformed the baseline Mamba across all tested context lengths (2k to 9k), extending Mamba's efficient context length to 6k tokens from the baseline's 4k.
ReMamba maintained comparable inference speeds to vanilla Mamba and incurred only minimal additional memory cost, significantly outperforming Transformer models in efficiency for long sequences.
solution: ReMamba employs a novel two-stage re-forward process that combines selective compression and selective adaptation to process long sequences efficiently.

In the selective compression stage, importance scores (cosine similarity with the final hidden state) identify and select top-K crucial hidden states from a specific context range, reconstructing a shorter effective input sequence.

During selective adaptation, these importance scores are dynamically integrated into Mamba's discrete time step (∆) calculation, allowing more critical compressed information to exert a stronger influence on the state space update.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Peking University and Meituan introduce ReMamba, a method that equips Mamba with a two-stage re-forward process for effective long-sequence modeling. This approach significantly boosts Mamba's performance on long-context benchmarks, achieving an average score of 27.86 on LongBench-E (a 3.23-point improvement over the baseline) while maintaining its linear time complexity and constant memory usage.

title: ReMamba: Equip Mamba with Effective Long-Sequence Modeling

abstract:
Structured state space models' (SSMs) development in recent studies, such as
Mamba and Mamba2, outperformed and solved the computational inefficiency of
transformers and large language models at small to medium scale. In this work,
we introduce the concept of controllability and observability to the original
Mamba SSM's architecture in our Sparse-Mamba (S-Mamba) for natural language
processing (NLP) applications. Moreover, we reinforce stability on the $nxn$
$A$ matrix on Mmaba2. The Mamba SSMs architecture drops the need for attention
layers or multilayer perception blocks in transformers. However, current Mamba
models lack reinforcement of controllability in state-space equations for
computing the $A$, $B$, $C$, and $D$ matrices at each time step, leading to
increased complexity and computational costs. Furthermore, the $A$ matrix in
Mamba2 is not always stable. We demonstrate a reduction of parameters compared
to the first published Mamba and Mamba2. We showcase an improvement in
perplexity by 5\% and a decrease in training time by 3\% after reinforcing
controllability and observability on the original Mamba architecture in our
proposed S-Mamba. We further enforce stability on the $A$ matrix in Mamba2 to
improve the loss and perplexity of the model. The controllable and stable $n
\times n$ state matrix $A$ is sparse, and it has only $n$ free parameters. Our
novel approach will ensure controllable/observable and stable SSMs, which will
be the gate key for Mamba3.
title: Sparse Mamba: Introducing Controllability, Observability, And Stability  To Structural State Space Models

abstract: The quadratic complexity of the attention mechanism in Transformer models has motivated the development of alternative architectures with sub-quadratic scaling, such as state-space models. Among these, Mamba has emerged as a leading architecture, achieving state-of-the-art results across a range of language modeling tasks. However, Mamba's performance significantly deteriorates when applied to contexts longer than those seen during pre-training, revealing a sharp sensitivity to context length extension. Through detailed analysis, we attribute this limitation to the out-of-distribution behaviour of its state-space dynamics, particularly within the parameterization of the state transition matrix $\mathbf{A}$. Unlike recent works which attribute this sensitivity to the vanished accumulation of discretization time steps, $\exp(-\sum_{t=1}^N\Delta_t)$, we establish a connection between state convergence behavior as the input length approaches infinity and the spectrum of the transition matrix $\mathbf{A}$, offering a well-founded explanation of its role in length extension. Next, to overcome this challenge, we propose an approach that applies spectrum scaling to pre-trained Mamba models to enable robust long-context generalization by selectively modulating the spectrum of $\mathbf{A}$ matrices in each layer. We show that this can significantly improve performance in settings where simply modulating $\Delta_t$ fails, validating our insights and providing avenues for better length generalization of state-space models with structured transition matrices.
title: Mamba Modulation: On the Length Generalization of Mamba

abstract: Recent advancements in State Space Models (SSMs) have attracted significant interest, particularly in models optimized for parallel training and handling long-range dependencies. Architectures like Mamba have scaled to billions of parameters with selective SSM. To facilitate broader applications using Mamba, exploring its efficiency is crucial. While token reduction techniques offer a straightforward post-training strategy, we find that applying existing methods directly to SSMs leads to substantial performance drops. Through insightful analysis, we identify the reasons for this failure and the limitations of current techniques. In response, we propose a tailored, unified post-training token reduction method for SSMs. Our approach integrates token importance and similarity, thus taking advantage of both pruning and merging, to devise a fine-grained intra-layer token reduction strategy. Extensive experiments show that our method improves the average accuracy by 5.7% to 13.1% on six benchmarks with Mamba-2 compared to existing methods, while significantly reducing computational demands and memory requirements.

keyInsights: The inherent differences in information flow between attention-based Transformers and sequential, state-based SSMs necessitate a specialized approach for token reduction.
A token importance metric derived from the aggregated clipped values of hidden states is more effective for SSMs than metrics typically used for attention mechanisms.

Combining importance-based token classification with a hybrid pruning/merging strategy, applied hierarchically and with fine-grained control for different architectural components, is crucial for maintaining SSM performance during reduction.
originalProblem: State Space Models (SSMs), like Mamba, show promise for efficient processing of long sequences but are hampered by efficiency concerns, especially at larger scales.
Existing token reduction techniques, effective for Transformer models, fail to maintain performance when directly applied to SSMs, leading to substantial accuracy drops.
The sequential computation of SSMs means information loss from pruning is amplified, while current merging methods often lack token importance considerations, both causing degradation.
results: UTRC significantly outperformed state-of-the-art token reduction baselines (PuMer, EViT) across various Mamba models (1.3B-2.8B parameters) on common sense reasoning benchmarks.
Achieved up to 13.1% higher average accuracy than the best baseline for Mamba-2-2.7B at 30% FLOPS reduction, maintaining reasonable perplexity on LAMBADA while baselines suffered severe degradation.
Demonstrated substantial efficiency improvements, including up to 40% GPU peak memory reduction and up to 1.37x inference throughput improvement for Mamba models at 30% FLOPS reduction.
solution: Developed Unified Token Reduction by Token Importance Classification (UTRC), a post-training token reduction method explicitly designed for SSMs, addressing the architectural differences from Transformers.
Proposed a novel token importance metric derived from the clipped values of SSM hidden states, which offers a finer-grained analysis of information distribution.

Implemented a unified strategy that classifies tokens by importance, then applies a hybrid approach of pruning and merging to less important tokens, while exclusively using merging for residual connections to preserve integrity.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers introduce Unified Token Reduction by Token Importance Classification (UTRC), the first post-training token reduction method specifically for State Space Models (SSMs). UTRC consistently outperforms existing token reduction baselines by up to 13.1% in average accuracy while achieving up to 40% GPU peak memory reduction and 1.37x throughput improvement for Mamba models.

title: Rethinking Token Reduction for State Space Models

abstract: State space models (SSMs) like Mamba have recently attracted much attention. Compared to Transformer-based large language models (LLMs), Mamba achieves linear computation complexity with the sequence length and demonstrates superior performance. However, Mamba is hard to accelerate due to the scattered activation outliers and the complex computation dependency, rendering existing LLM accelerators inefficient. In this paper, we propose LightMamba that co-designs the quantization algorithm and FPGA accelerator architecture for efficient Mamba inference. We first propose an FPGA-friendly post-training quantization algorithm that features rotation-assisted quantization and power-of-two SSM quantization to reduce the majority of computation to 4-bit. We further design an FPGA accelerator that partially unrolls the Mamba computation to balance the efficiency and hardware costs. Through computation reordering as well as fine-grained tiling and fusion, the hardware utilization and memory efficiency of the accelerator get drastically improved. We implement LightMamba on Xilinx Versal VCK190 FPGA and achieve 4.65x to 6.06x higher energy efficiency over the GPU baseline. When evaluated on Alveo U280 FPGA, LightMamba reaches 93 tokens/s, which is 1.43x that of the GPU baseline. Our code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Rotation-assisted quantization, when selectively applied and fused with neighboring operations, effectively mitigates scattered activation outliers in Mamba's linear layers for 4-bit precision.

Power-of-Two quantization for the SSM layer enables re-quantization using simple bit-shifting operations, significantly reducing DSP and LUT costs associated with element-wise multiplications on FPGAs.

Computation reordering and fine-grained tiling and fusion dramatically improve FPGA hardware utilization from 58% to 96% and reduce on-chip memory (URAM) usage by 4x, respectively, by optimizing dataflow and reducing intermediate storage.
originalProblem: Mamba models exhibit scattered activation outliers, making low-bit quantization (e.g., 4-bit) challenging and leading to significant accuracy degradation.
The core SSM layer's element-wise computations are costly in floating-point precision on FPGAs, while direct quantization incurs substantial re-quantization overhead.
The complex computation graph and data dependencies within Mamba hinder efficient spatial unrolling, pipelining, and effective on-chip memory utilization on FPGAs.
results: Achieved W4A4 quantization for the entire Mamba model (LightMamba*) with minimal accuracy degradation, outperforming prior methods with a perplexity of 6.35 on LAMBADA.

Demonstrated superior energy efficiency, with LightMamba achieving 6.06x higher energy efficiency than an NVIDIA RTX 2070 and 4.65x higher than an RTX 4090, while providing 1.43x average throughput improvement over an RTX 2070.

Optimizations led to a 32% reduction in total latency and increased hardware utilization from 58% to 96% through computation reordering, alongside a 4x reduction in URAM usage via fine-grained tiling and fusion.
solution: Proposed an FPGA-friendly rotation-assisted post-training quantization for linear layers to handle scattered outliers and Power-of-Two (PoT) quantization for the SSM layer, enabling efficient re-quantization.

Designed a partially unfolded spatial FPGA architecture with custom hardware units: a Matrix Multiplication Unit (MMU), a fully pipelined SSM Unit (SSMU), and a dedicated Hadamard Transform Unit (HTU).
Implemented computation reordering to allow coarse-grained pipelining between layers and fine-grained tiling and fusion within the SSMU to minimize intermediate storage and improve efficiency.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Peking University developed LightMamba, the first FPGA-based acceleration framework for the Mamba model, utilizing a co-design approach for quantization and hardware. This work enables 4-bit weight and activation quantization for Mamba with minimal accuracy loss, achieving 4.65-6.06x higher energy efficiency than GPUs and a 1.43x average throughput increase.

title: LightMamba: Efficient Mamba Acceleration on FPGA with Quantization and Hardware Co-design

abstract: Structured State Space Models (SSMs) have emerged as compelling alternatives to Transformer architectures, offering linear-time complexity and superior performance in various sequence modeling tasks. Despite their advantages, SSMs like the original Mamba-2 face training difficulties due to the sensitivities introduced by the extended series of recurrent matrix multiplications. In this paper, we propose an advanced architecture that mitigates these challenges by decomposing A-multiplications into multiple groups and optimizing positional encoding through Grouped Finite Impulse Response (FIR) filtering. This new structure, denoted as Grouped FIR-enhanced SSM (GFSSM), employs semiseparable matrices for efficient computation. Furthermore, inspired by the "attention sink" phenomenon identified in streaming language models, we incorporate a similar mechanism to enhance the stability and performance of our model over extended sequences. Our approach further bridges the gap between SSMs and Transformer architectures, offering a viable path forward for scalable and high-performing sequence modeling.
title: Enhanced Structured State Space Models via Grouped FIR Filtering and Attention Sink Mechanisms

abstract: Diffusion language models (DLMs) have emerged as a promising alternative to autoregressive (AR) generation, yet their reliance on Transformer backbones limits inference efficiency due to quadratic attention or KV-cache overhead. We introduce DiffuMamba, a masked diffusion language model built on a bidirectional Mamba backbone that combines the diffusion objective with linear-time sequence modeling, and DiffuMamba-H, a hybrid variant with interleaved attention. Across scales up to 1.3B parameters, our models match Transformer-based diffusion in downstream performance while achieving up to 8.2x and 4.3x higher inference throughput, respectively, on long sequences. We further present a systematic analysis of inference efficiency across modern DLM variants combining asymptotic complexity with empirical measurements. Notably, cache-efficient block diffusion with Mamba mixers emerges as the only strategy that scales linearly with sequence length and achieves the strongest performance across all baselines, suggesting a promising direction for future diffusion-based generation systems.
title: DiffuMamba: High-Throughput Diffusion LMs with Mamba Backbone

abstract:
State Space Models (SSMs) have become serious contenders in the field of
sequential modeling, challenging the dominance of Transformers. At the same
time, Mixture of Experts (MoE) has significantly improved Transformer-based
Large Language Models, including recent state-of-the-art open models. We
propose that to unlock the potential of SSMs for scaling, they should be
